title: Demonstration of Exponential Quantum Speedup with Constant-Depth Compiled Circuits for Simon's Problem
authors: Phattharaporn Singkanipa, Victor Kasatkin, Daniel A. Lidar
abstract: We demonstrate exponential quantum speedup for a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem on present-day superconducting quantum processors by introducing a hardware-aware compilation strategy that compiles the quantum part of each Simon query circuit to constant depth. The resulting compiled circuits have $O(1)$ depth and linear connectivity, map directly onto common device layouts, and avoid additional routing and SWAP overhead. Implemented on IBM's $156$-qubit Boston and $120$-qubit Miami processors, the resulting circuits achieve sufficiently high fidelity to exhibit algorithmic quantum speedup without error suppression. Using the number-of-queries-to-solution metric, we observe exponential speedup over the classical lower bound across the full Hamming-weight range studied on Boston and across low-to-intermediate Hamming weights on Miami; at higher Hamming weights on Miami, we still observe polynomial speedup. The same construction also reaches a regime where the original Simon problem is recovered for the problem sizes studied. These results show that careful hardware-aware compilation can make exponential quantum speedup experimentally accessible for a canonical hidden-subgroup problem in the NISQ regime.
summary: This paper demonstrates exponential quantum speedup for Simon's problem on real superconducting hardware using a new constant-depth compilation strategy. By making the circuits shallow enough for the NISQ era, the authors show that algorithmic advantage is experimentally accessible even without full error correction.
main_problem: Demonstrating exponential quantum speedup for a version of Simon's problem on NISQ-era superconducting hardware by overcoming high circuit depth and SWAP overhead.
main_result: The authors achieved exponential quantum speedup on the IBM Boston processor across a wide Hamming-weight range and observed a transition from exponential to polynomial speedup on the IBM Miami processor at higher Hamming weights.
method: A hardware-aware compilation strategy was developed to reduce the quantum part of the Simon query circuit to constant depth (O(1)) and linear connectivity, mapping directly to the device layout.
model_or_system: The experiment was performed on IBM superconducting quantum processors, specifically the 156-qubit 'Boston' (heavy-hex connectivity) and 120-qubit 'Miami' (square-lattice connectivity) devices.
key_observables: Number of queries to solution (NTS), success probability (Pr(z . b = 0)), and scaling exponents (alpha for polylog and beta for poly models).
important_parameters: Problem size (n), Hamming weight of the hidden bitstring (w), and the scaling regimes (exponential vs. polynomial).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem and utilizes a factorized-CPTP-map assumption to neglect crosstalk between qubit groups.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the quantum circuit construction, the reduction of oracle complexity, scaling plots of NTS vs. problem size, and statistical comparisons (AIC/R2) between polylog and poly models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem and hardware-aware compilation method, details the oracle construction and circuit depth reduction, presents experimental results on Boston and Miami processors, performs statistical model selection (polylog vs. poly), and discusses the impact of optimization tools like Q-CTRL.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Comparison of two oracle constructions for Simon-5 (b = 01111) in the graph representation. CNOTs can be placed in the same entangling layer when they do not share any qubits. For the HW(b) = 4 instance shown here, the construction presented in this work can be scheduled in two entangling layers, whereas the con- struction in Ref. [47] requires three under the same non-overlapping- qubit scheduling rule.

FIG. 3. Circuit construction underlying the compiled Simon-5 query circuits in the graph representation. Using the reduction procedure, we can trace out an appropriate subset of the leading dashed boxes to obtain Simon-m for any m satisfying HW(b) ≤m < 5.

FIG. 5. log2(NTSQ) as a function of n (bottom axis) for the restricted-HW Simon problem with cutoff w = 3 on Boston (blue, up to n = 63) and Miami (red, up to n = 60); the top axis shows log2(Nw). The solid lines denote fits using the polylog model [Eq. (8)], and the dashed lines denote fits using the poly model [Eq. (9)]. The error bars show bootstrapped 1σ uncertainties. The yellow dash-dot lines represent the scaling of log2(NTSlb C ) and also serve as a visual guide for the large-n scaling of the poly model. The gray dashed line is log2 of the interpolated noiseless-quantum esti- mate given by Eq. (6).

FIG. 6. log2(NTSQ) as a function of n on Boston (blue) and Miami (red) for the original wnSimon-n, up to n = 20 and n = 16, respec- tively. The line styles and reference curves are the same as in Fig. 5.

FIG. 7. Test of whether the polylog or poly model is the better fit for NTSQ as a function of HW w. For both Boston (blue) and Miami (red), solid lines with diamond markers denote the unrestricted Simon’s problem wnSimon-n with n ≤w, and dashed lines with circle markers denote the restricted-HW Simon’s problem wwSimon-n. The maximum HW is wmax = 16 and wmax = 20 on Miami and Boston, respectively. For the restricted-HW version, w ∈[1, wmax]; each point is fitted for n ∈[2, nmax], where nmax is the largest problem size for which the bar height in Fig. 9 is at least w. For the unrestricted version, w ∈[7, wmax]; the lower bound choice w = 7 is discussed in the main text, and each point is...

FIG. 8. The fitted scaling exponent β of the poly model as a func- tion of Hamming weight w on Miami for the unrestricted-HW case (solid diamonds, shown for w ∈[10, 16]) and the restricted-HW case (circles, shown for w ∈[12, 16]). In both cases, the fitted quantum exponent satisfies βQ < βC = 1/2 throughout the displayed range, indicating polynomial quantum speedup wherever the poly model is the preferred fit. Error bars represent one standard deviation (1σ) of the fitted parameter obtained from bootstrapped data.

FIG. 9. Availability range of the NTS analysis as a function of problem size. For each problem size n shown, the bar height gives the largest Hamming-weight cutoff wavail max (n) for which NTSQ could be evaluated on Boston (blue) and Miami (red). A solid bar reaching wavail max (n) = n corresponds to the original Simon problem wnSimon-n; a cross-hatched bar with wavail max (n) < n indicates that only restricted-HW instances wwSimon-n were available at that problem size. For example, on Miami the n = 17 unrestricted-Simon instance did not yield a usable NTSQ value, whereas the w18Simon-18 instance did. FIG. 12. Probabilities of obtaining a valid equation, Pr(z ⋅b = 0) (cross markers) and a valid nonzero equation, Pr(z ⋅b = 0 ∧z ≠0) (open-circle markers) on Miami (red) and Boston (blue), as functions of problem size n = 2, 3, . . . , nmax, where nmax = 60 (Miami) and nmax = 65 (Boston). Each data point is averaged over Hamming- weight classes i = 1, 2, . . . , n with weights proportional to (n i). The probability Pr(z ⋅b = 0) serves as a direct measure of circuit perfor- mance, since an ideal Simon circuit has Pr(z ⋅b = 0) = 1.
FIG. 13. Probability of obtaining z ⋅b = 0 as a function of problem size n, reproducing the results of Fig. 12 with the addition of data obtained via Q-CTRL.
